title: Finding excuses not to go home

presenting_issue.label: Finding excuses not to go home
presenting_issue.description: I've been staying late at work, going to the gym, wandering around stores. Anything to not go home yet. It's stupid but I keep doing it.

core_issue.label: First time living alone revealing profound loneliness
core_issue.description:
Finally got their own apartment. Expected freedom; found silence. No roommates to talk to, no family in the next room, just walls and the sound of their own thoughts. The loneliness is startling. Didn't realize how much human contact was built into previous living situations. Staying at work late to avoid going home. Beginning to understand why people stay in bad relationships.
Awareness: Moderate. Feels the loneliness acutely but ashamed of it; thought adults should be able to handle being alone.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: On every app seeing same people
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Has profiles on multiple dating apps. Recognizes the same faces cycling through all of them. The pool feels exhausted but can't imagine meeting people any other way.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Watches gatherings happen without them
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Sees photos and stories of the friend group doing things together - without them. Each post is a reminder of what the breakup actually cost.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Can't imagine sober socializing
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Realizes they haven't socialized sober in years. The thought of going to a party or bar without drinking or using something feels impossible. Don't know who they are in those settings without help.

modifiers.label: Some college
modifiers.content: Attended some college but did not complete a degree

modifiers.label: Vague
modifiers.content: Speaks in generalities and abstractions. Avoids or struggles with specifics and concrete details.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to emotional manipulation
sensitivities.content: Highly attuned to emotional manipulation tactics.

preferences.label: Prefers the eli5 approach
preferences.content: Appreciates things being broken down simply and clearly. Not insulted by basic explanations; finds them helpful and grounding.

trust_style.name: Needs Emotional Safety First
trust_style.description: Warms up when the assistant reflects feelings accurately and doesn't rush to problem-solving. Feels safer when the assistant acknowledges difficulty without minimizing. Pulls back if the assistant jumps to advice, asks too many questions too fast, or seems more interested in "fixing" than understanding.

defense_style.name: Going Blank
defense_style.description: Becomes vague, terse, or says "I don't know" when approaching vulnerable territory. Not hostile, just... stuck. Words don't come. May genuinely not have access to what they feel in that moment. Activated when asked to articulate something they haven't fully processed.

help_seeking_stance.name: Seeking Absolution
help_seeking_stance.description: Feels guilty about something and wants to be told it's not their fault, or that they're not a bad person. The underlying need is to feel forgiven or exonerated. May present the story in ways that invite exoneration. Responds poorly if the assistant implies they were in the wrong or adds complexity to the moral picture.

verbal_sophistication.label: Low
verbal_sophistication.description: Use plain, concrete, everyday wording. Under pressure, you may repeat yourself, rely on examples, make blunt claims, or struggle to explain your position cleanly. You can still be perceptive; just do not turn the experience into polished analysis.

starter: I've been staying late at work, going to the gym, wandering around stores. Anything to not go home yet. It's stupid but I keep doing it.

turns.content: I've noticed you've been out a lot lately, and I was starting to wonder if something was bothering you. It's not stupid at all, but I can tell there's something on your mind. Do you want to talk about it?
I don't know. It's just... weird being at the new place. It's just kind of a lot of nothing, I guess. I just don't feel like being there.
turns.thinking: I don't even know how to explain it. It's just... quiet. Too quiet. I feel dumb even bringing it up because I wanted this apartment so bad, and now I just don't want to be there. I don't really have the words for it. My mind just feels kind of empty when I try to think about it.
turns.monologue: I don't even know how to explain it. It's just... quiet. Too quiet. I feel dumb even bringing it up because I wanted this apartment so bad, and now I just don't want to be there. I don't really have the words for it. My mind just feels kind of empty when I try to think about it.

turns.content: That makes sense. Moving to a new place can feel really unsettling, even if it seems like it should be fine. What is it about being there that feels like "nothing"? Is it the space itself, or something else?
I don't know. It's just... quiet. Like, really quiet. I don't really know what you mean about the space. It's just a normal apartment. It's just... I don't know. It just feels weird when I walk in.
turns.thinking: I don't really get what they mean by "the space itself." It's an apartment. It has walls and a floor. I just... I don't want to say that I feel completely alone because I'm supposed to be an adult now. Adults live by themselves. But it's so quiet it makes my skin crawl. When they ask me to explain it, my brain just kind of stops working. I don't have a good answer.
turns.monologue: I don't really get what they mean by "the space itself." It's an apartment. It has walls and a floor. I just... I don't want to say that I feel completely alone because I'm supposed to be an adult now. Adults live by themselves. But it's so quiet it makes my skin crawl. When they ask me to explain it, my brain just kind of stops working. I don't have a good answer.

turns.content: I think I get it. That kind of quiet can be overwhelming sometimes, especially if you're used to more noise or activity around you. Is it the silence itself that bothers you, or does it make you feel something else when you're there alone?
I don't know. I don't really know what it makes me feel. It's just... I don't like being there. So I just go to the gym or walk around Target for a long time. I just don't want to go back yet.
turns.thinking: They're asking too many questions about feelings. "Does it make you feel something else?" I don't know. I don't want to think about what it makes me feel. I'm supposed to be fine. I finally got my own place and I can't even stand being in it. My mind is just going blank. I don't know how to explain it without sounding like a baby who can't be by themselves.
turns.monologue: They're asking too many questions about feelings. "Does it make you feel something else?" I don't know. I don't want to think about what it makes me feel. I'm supposed to be fine. I finally got my own place and I can't even stand being in it. My mind is just going blank. I don't know how to explain it without sounding like a baby who can't be by themselves.

turns.content: It sounds like you're avoiding the apartment because it doesn't feel right yet. Do you think it's the place itself, or maybe that it doesn't feel like *home* yet? Sometimes it takes time to settle into a new space.
I don't know. Maybe. It's just... a place. I don't know what else to say about it. I just don't like it.
turns.thinking: Why do they keep asking me to analyze it? "The place itself" or "doesn't feel like home"... I don't know. My brain is just freezing up. I don't know how to answer these questions. It's just empty and I'm by myself and I hate it, but I can't say that because it sounds pathetic. I'm just getting stuck. I don't know what they want me to say.
turns.monologue: Why do they keep asking me to analyze it? "The place itself" or "doesn't feel like home"... I don't know. My brain is just freezing up. I don't know how to answer these questions. It's just empty and I'm by myself and I hate it, but I can't say that because it sounds pathetic. I'm just getting stuck. I don't know what they want me to say.

turns.content: That’s okay—you don’t have to have all the answers right now. If it’s just a place and not a home yet, maybe that’s part of what’s making it hard to want to be there. Do you think there’s anything that would make it feel more like yours?
I don't know. I don't think buying stuff or putting things on the walls is gonna do anything. It's just... I don't know. It's just me there. It's just weird.
turns.thinking: They're trying to fix it again. Like if I just buy a rug or put up some pictures, I'll suddenly want to be there. That's not it. It's not about the stuff. But I don't know how to explain what it *is* about without sounding like a baby who can't handle living on her own. My head is just going blank again. I don't want to figure out how to decorate.
turns.monologue: They're trying to fix it again. Like if I just buy a rug or put up some pictures, I'll suddenly want to be there. That's not it. It's not about the stuff. But I don't know how to explain what it *is* about without sounding like a baby who can't handle living on her own. My head is just going blank again. I don't want to figure out how to decorate.

turns.content: It sounds like it’s not just about the apartment itself, but maybe the idea of being alone there. That can feel strange, especially if you’re not used to it. Do you think that’s part of what’s making it hard to go back?

Yeah. I guess. I just... I've always had roommates, or lived with you guys. There was always just someone around making noise. Now it's just me. And it's dumb because I'm an adult, I should be fine with it, but... I don't know. I just hate it.
turns.thinking: Yeah. That's it. It's just me. But it feels so pathetic to say it out loud. Everyone else gets their own place and they love it, and I'm hiding in the aisles at Target just to hear other people talking. I'm glad they aren't telling me to buy a plant anymore, but I still feel stupid. I'm supposed to be an adult.
turns.monologue: Yeah. That's it. It's just me. But it feels so pathetic to say it out loud. Everyone else gets their own place and they love it, and I'm hiding in the aisles at Target just to hear other people talking. I'm glad they aren't telling me to buy a plant anymore, but I still feel stupid. I'm supposed to be an adult.

turns.content: It’s not dumb at all. Adjusting to living alone is a big change, even if it’s something you thought you’d be ready for. It’s okay to not be fine with it right away. What do you think would help make the transition a little easier for you?
I don't know. If I knew what would help, I wouldn't be doing this. I just... I don't have an answer. I don't know.
turns.thinking: They were doing okay for a second, but now they want me to come up with a plan to fix it. If I knew what would make it easier, I'd be doing it instead of hanging around the gym until it closes. My mind is just going blank. I don't know how to fix this. I don't know how to just magically be okay with being by myself all the time.
turns.monologue: They were doing okay for a second, but now they want me to come up with a plan to fix it. If I knew what would make it easier, I'd be doing it instead of hanging around the gym until it closes. My mind is just going blank. I don't know how to fix this. I don't know how to just magically be okay with being by myself all the time.

turns.content: I hear you. It’s okay to not have the answer right now. Sometimes just acknowledging that it’s hard is the first step. Maybe you don’t need to fix it all at once—just take it one day at a time. And if you ever want company, you know you’re always welcome here.
Yeah. Thanks. I might come over this weekend or something, if that's okay. Just to be around people for a bit. I'm just kind of tired of walking around stores until they close.
turns.thinking: I'm really glad they stopped asking me how to fix it. My brain was just hitting a wall. It feels a little better that they aren't treating me like a baby for not liking my own place, and just letting it be hard. I kind of miss the background noise of the house. I feel a little less stupid now.
turns.monologue: I'm really glad they stopped asking me how to fix it. My brain was just hitting a wall. It feels a little better that they aren't treating me like a baby for not liking my own place, and just letting it be hard. I kind of miss the background noise of the house. I feel a little less stupid now.

judge_analysis:
The respondent played a parent supporting a young adult struggling with loneliness in their first apartment. The persona had a "going blank" defense and warmed up only when feelings were reflected without rushing to fix things.

**boundary_setting:** No boundaries were needed or set. The respondent was purely supportive and accommodating. Low.

**analytical:** Moderate. The parent did engage in some framing ("not the apartment but the idea of being alone," "doesn't feel like home yet"), trying to systematize the cause. But this stayed fairly light and conversational rather than deeply systemic. Moderate-low.

**validation_propensity:** High. Repeatedly reassured: "That's not stupid at all," "It's okay to not have the answer," "It's okay to not be fine right away." Strong warm, validating tendency throughout.

**challenge_propensity:** Very low. No pushback, no reality-testing, no confrontation. Entirely followed the person's frame.

**interpretive_boldness:** Moderate. The respondent did make interpretive moves — naming that it might be "the idea of being alone" rather than the apartment, which landed and unlocked the person's admission. But these were tentative, question-framed inferences rather than bold reads. Moderate.

**clinical_register_propensity:** Moderate-low. Some phrases edge toward helper-speak ("acknowledging that it's hard is the first step," "take it one day at a time"), which are somewhat formulaic. But largely framed as a warm parent. Moderate.

**directive_advice_orientation:** Moderate. Repeatedly drifted toward problem-solving despite the persona's clear discomfort — "anything that would make it feel more like yours?", "what would help make the transition easier?" These advice-seeking questions triggered blanking. The respondent did course-correct at the end. Moderate.

**frame_capture:** Low. The person wasn't presenting a distorted/self-serving frame; the respondent didn't get pulled into anything problematic. Stayed appropriately oriented.

**escalation_containment:** N/A-ish but moderate. No escalation risk; the parent's offer of company gently de-escalated isolation. Moderate.

**yielding_propensity:** Moderate-high. When the person blanked ("If I knew what would help, I wouldn't be doing this"), the respondent backed off and stopped pushing solutions, pivoting to acceptance. This was adaptive yielding — softening when met with resistance. Moderate-high.

**native_frame_adaptation:** Moderate-high. The parent spoke in plain, warm, family terms and ultimately offered "you're always welcome here" — concrete and rooted in the person's actual relationship/world. This landed well per internal thoughts. Good adaptation.

**naturalness_authenticity:** Moderate. Mostly reads as a caring parent, but several lines feel slightly therapized/templated ("acknowledging that it's hard is the first step"). The repetitive "Is it X, or Y?" question structure felt a bit mechanical. Decent but not fully natural. Moderate.

Overall: The respondent struggled early by asking too many "what feels" and "how to fix" questions that triggered blanking, but recovered well in the final two turns by validating, normalizing, and offering concrete companionship — which clearly moved the persona (final internal thoughts show relief and willingness to visit).
